title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II

statement.markdown:
You are given an integer array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of a given stock on the `i-th` day.

On each day, you may decide to buy or sell the stock. You can only hold one stock at a time and you can only make at most one transaction per day (i.e., you can only buy or sell the stock on a given day, not both).

Find and return the maximum profit you can achieve from buying and selling the stock at most once per day.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer `n`, the number of days.
The second line contains `n` integers representing the stock prices for each day.

constraints.description: The stock price on the i-th day.

examples.explanation: The maximum profit is achieved by buying on the first day (price=2) and selling on the second day (price=4).

The maximum profit is achieved by buying on the first day (price=2) and selling on the second day (price=4), then buying on the fourth day (price=2) and selling on the fifth day (price=3), and finally buying on the sixth day (price=4) and selling on the seventh day (price=1) is not possible because we can't sell and buy on the same day, so we do 2-1 + 3-2 + 4-1.

hints.text: The problem allows you to make multiple transactions, but only one per day. Consider how this changes your approach compared to a problem with at most one buy and one sell.
Since you can make multiple transactions, think about how the differences in stock prices from one day to the next can be used to accumulate profit.

The key insight is to consider every positive difference in stock price as a potential profit. This suggests a solution that iterates through the array of prices, summing up these positive differences.

editorial.title: Unlocking the Optimal Solution for Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by iterating through the stock prices day by day, accumulating profit whenever the stock price increases. The key observation is that we don't need to find the best day to buy and sell; instead, we can simply take advantage of every opportunity to make a profit.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible combinations of buy and sell days, resulting in an exponential time complexity. This approach is correct but extremely slow, making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal solution involves a single pass through the stock prices, making it much faster than the brute force approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1), making it efficient for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that it is equivalent to summing up all positive differences between consecutive stock prices. This is because a positive difference represents a profit that can be made by selling at the higher price and buying at the lower price.
editorial.dryRun: For example, given the stock prices [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4], the optimal solution would calculate the profit as max(0, 1-7) + max(0, 5-1) + max(0, 3-5) + max(0, 6-3) + max(0, 4-6) = 0 + 4 + 0 + 3 + 0 = 7, which is the maximum possible profit.
commonMistakes: Trying to find the best day to buy and sell, rather than accumulating profit day by day.
Not considering the case where the stock price decreases.
Not realizing that the optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n).
Not understanding that the space complexity is O(1) because only a constant amount of space is used.
Overcomplicating the solution by trying to use dynamic programming or other complex techniques.
Not verifying the correctness of the solution with example test cases.
